preFix.title: Replacing a sash window cord
preFix.subtitle: Remove the staff bead and parting bead, take out the sash, and restring with new waxed cord
preFix.excerpt: How to replace a broken or frayed sash cord on a traditional box sash window. Covers removing both sashes without damage, replacing the lead weights and cord, and refitting the beads so the window runs smoothly.
preFix.sourceNotes: Newey & Drage, Practical Carpentry and Joinery. Collins Complete DIY Manual.

content.text: Box sash windows are self-repairing in the sense that every part is designed to be taken apart and put back. The process is systematic and largely about keeping track of which parts go where, the sashes, weights, and beads all look similar once they are out.
Step 1, Remove the staff bead
Score along the paint line between the
staff bead

and the wall with the utility knife. The paint will have glued the bead in place over multiple coats. Insert the chisel behind the bead and lever gently, work along the bead rather than levering at one point, to avoid splitting it. The bead is held by small nails; pulling it slowly from both ends keeps it intact for reuse.
Step 2, Remove the lower sash

With the staff beads off, the lower sash can be swung into the room and lowered. Cut the old cords at the sash grooves. Tie a knot in each hanging cord end before it disappears up into the frame, the weight will otherwise drop to the bottom of the cavity and the cord will be very hard to retrieve.
Step 3, Remove the parting bead and upper sash
Prise out the
parting bead
from the groove with the chisel, working carefully, it is thin. Lower the upper sash and cut its cords in the same way, knotting each end.
Step 4, Open the pockets and retrieve the weights
Open the
pockets

, small painted-over panels in the lower side of the frame. Unscrew or prise open. Remove all four weights. Clean the inside of the frame cavity with a vacuum. Check the weights: they should be approximately equal in pairs (inner weights for the upper sash are typically slightly lighter than outer weights for the lower sash).
Step 5, Thread new cord and rehang
Cut new
sash cord

to the same length as the old cords: sit the sash at the bottom, pull the weight to the top of the cavity, and add 200 mm for the knot and the groove. Feed a small 'mouse' (a bent wire or a heavy nail tied to a piece of string) over the pulley and down to the pocket opening, then tie the cord to the string and pull it over the pulley. Tie the cord to the weight with a figure-of-eight knot. Replace the weights, close the pockets, and nail the cord into the sash groove with two 25 mm cut nails.
Step 6, Refit beads and test

Hang the upper sash first, then replace the parting bead. Hang the lower sash and replace the staff beads, nailing through the existing nail holes where possible. Test both sashes for free running and balanced counterweight: a properly balanced sash stays where it is placed without sliding down.
Troubleshooter

items.fix: Add a small extra weight (a piece of lead sheet from a plumber's merchant) to the lighter end. Cut new cord to the correct length.
items.cause: Weights too light for the sash, or new cord cut too short so the weight does not travel far enough to counterbalance

items.fix: Remove and re-nail the staff bead 1 to 2 mm further back from the sash face.
items.cause: Staff bead nailed too tight against the sash face

initialFindings.message: PATTERN tutorial has no Method/Steps section and no orderedList — sequential instructions missing
